**Curry leaf** or **kadi patta** is an herb common to South and Southeast Asian cooking.[[1]](#cite_note-:4-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3)

## Characteristics

[edit | edit source]

The leaf is small and green, with a pointed tip and glossy appearance.[[1]](#cite_note-:4-1)[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3)[[4]](#cite_note-:2-4) The flavor is described as complexly aromatic, with notes of citrus, sulfur, pine, bay, and more.[[5]](#cite_note-:3-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[4]](#cite_note-:2-4) The leaves are sometimes dried and even ground, though the flavor is less than that of fresh leaves.[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3)[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)

## Selection and storage

[edit | edit source]

When purchasing fresh leaves (ideal), select those that are small and nicely green, without blemishes or rot.[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3)*[[5]](#cite_note-:3-5)* They can be wrapped in paper towels and stored in the fridge in a plastic container for 1–2 weeks or so.[[4]](#cite_note-:2-4)[[5]](#cite_note-:3-5) They can also be stored in the freezer for at least a couple months.[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3)[[5]](#cite_note-:3-5) If you need dry leaves, drying them yourself can give you more control and gives you a better idea of their age.[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3)

## Use

[edit | edit source]

The leaf is used in many South Indian dishes,[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)[[4]](#cite_note-:2-4) where it adds flavor and a spicy pleasant smell. Typically, it is first fried in oil to help it bloom and release its aromatic compounds, as in *tadka*.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3)[[4]](#cite_note-:2-4) However, the whole leaf itself is rarely eaten.[[1]](#cite_note-:4-1)

## Substitution

[edit | edit source]

Unfortunately, it's very difficult to substitute another ingredient for curry leaf—you may simply want to leave them out if you can't find any.[[4]](#cite_note-:2-4)
